title: Aubergine parmigiana
subtitle: Southern Italian baked aubergine with tomato, mozzarella and Parmesan.
excerpt: Sliced aubergine, salted and fried until golden, layered with a simple tomato sauce, mozzarella and Parmesan, then baked until bubbling and set. A southern Italian classic that serves four comfortably.
sourceNotes: Melanzane alla parmigiana. The dish is documented throughout southern Italian culinary history; origin is contested between Sicily, Campania and Calabria. Ada Boni's Il Talismano della Felicità (1929) contains a foundational version. Despite the name, Parmesan cheese is from northern Italy; the name 'parmigiana' may derive from the Sicilian 'parmiciana' (wooden shutters), referring to the layered construction.

content.text: The effort in parmigiana is the frying. Salting the aubergine is not optional; it draws out moisture and firms the flesh so the slices hold together in the oil. Fry in batches without crowding the pan. The reward for the extra work is layers that stay distinct in the finished dish rather than collapsing into mush.
The tomato sauce should be barely cooked, just long enough to thicken slightly. Basil goes into each layer. The result should rest for 15 minutes after baking so it sets enough to cut cleanly.

items.name: Basil
items.prepNote: leaves picked; some for the sauce, some for layering

items.name: Mozzarella
items.prepNote: fresh, well drained, torn or sliced

content.text: Lay the aubergine slices on a tray. Sprinkle both sides with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Leave for 20 minutes, then pat thoroughly dry with kitchen paper.

Heat 2 tablespoons of olive oil in a saucepan over medium heat. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, a pinch of salt and a few basil leaves. Simmer for 15 minutes until slightly thickened.
Heat {{olive-oil}} of oil in a large frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry the aubergine slices in batches for 2 to 3 minutes per side until golden. Drain on kitchen paper.

Heat the oven to 190°C. Spread a thin layer of tomato sauce over the base of the baking dish. Add a layer of aubergine, then sauce, then {{mozzarella}} of mozzarella, a few basil leaves and a scattering of {{parmesan}} of Parmesan. Repeat until all the ingredients are used, finishing with sauce and a generous layer of Parmesan. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until bubbling and golden on top. Leave to rest for 15 minutes before cutting.

items.fix: Salting is non-negotiable. Drain mozzarella thoroughly and pat dry; it releases a lot of water as it bakes.
items.cause: Aubergine not salted, or mozzarella too wet.

items.fix: The oil must be hot before the slices go in. Aubergine absorbs oil aggressively in lukewarm oil; in hot oil, it seals quickly and absorbs much less.
items.cause: Aubergine cooked in oil that was not hot enough.

items.fix: Always rest for at least 15 minutes. If the sauce is very thin, cook it down for longer before layering.
items.cause: Not rested, or sauce too thin.

content.text: Parmigiana belongs to the summer table of southern Italy, made when aubergines are at their best in August. It is served at room temperature as much as hot, which makes it an ideal dish for a long lunch. Made a day ahead, the layers settle and the flavour deepens. Cold, sliced and eaten on bread the next morning, it is also excellent.
